title: Unit and Distinct Distances in Typical Norms
speaker: Noga Alon
abstract: Erdős' unit distance problem and his related distinct distances problem are arguably the best known open problems in discrete geometry.   They ask for the maximum possible number of unit distances, and the minimum possible number of distinct distances, respectively, determined by n points in the Euclidean plane. The same problems for normed spaces other than the Euclidean plane have been raised in the 1980s by Ulam and Erdős and attracted a lot of attention over the years. We give an essentially tight answer to both questions for almost all norms on Rd

proof_stages.label: Classical benchmarks and normed reformulation
proof_stages.summary: The talk introduces the Euclidean unit-distance, distinct-distance, and chromatic-number problems, then reformulates them for d-dimensional norms via centrally symmetric unit balls and reviews known extremal behavior motivating the typical-norm questions.

proof_stages.label: Baire-category notion of a typical norm
proof_stages.summary: The Hausdorff topology on compact unit balls is used to define nowhere dense and meager sets, so that 'most norms' means all norms outside a meager subset of the Baire space of norm unit balls.

proof_stages.label: Universal lower bounds and sharpness obstructions
proof_stages.summary: Subset-sum hypercube constructions show that every norm has order n log n unit distances, while line and equilateral-set considerations give unavoidable lower or small-size obstructions for distinct-distance statements.

proof_stages.label: Main typical-norm theorems
proof_stages.summary: For fixed dimension d, typical norms have at most (d/2)n log_2 n unit distances, this is close to sharp for every norm, typical norms force nearly n distinct distances, and typical planar norms have unit-distance chromatic number exactly four.

proof_stages.label: Reduction from many unit edges to rational spanning
proof_stages.summary: The proof of the unit-distance upper bound is reduced to showing that a graph with more than (d/2)n log_2 n unit edges forces a finite set of unit difference vectors to rationally span more than d times as many unit vectors.

proof_stages.label: Independent directions and grid isoperimetry
proof_stages.summary: When the distinct unit-difference directions are rationally independent, vertices can be coordinatized as a subgraph of a high-dimensional integer grid, where the discrete edge-isoperimetric inequality gives at most (1/2)n log_2 n edges.

proof_stages.label: Matroid partition of direction classes
proof_stages.summary: The matroid partition theorem implies that, unless an over-spanning set of directions exists, all unit directions split into at most d rationally independent classes, so summing the grid bounds yields the desired edge bound.

proof_stages.label: Generic avoidance and algebraic extensions
proof_stages.summary: For each fixed rational spanning pattern and separation scale, polytope approximation and generic perturbation of facet constants make the required linear relation impossible, and analogous algebraic/topological tools over fields generated by distance values underlie the distinct-distance theorem and the final open problems.
